accepted.question_text: Let A be a self-adjoint cyclic operator on a Hilbert space, let φ be a cyclic vector for A, and let A_alpha = A + alpha(·, φ)φ be a rank-one perturbation with spectral measure mu_alpha taken with respect to φ. Characterize the singular continuous part of mu_alpha in terms of the unperturbed spectral measure mu.
accepted.context_brief: Singular continuous spectrum under rank-one perturbation

evidence.quote: Let A be a self-adjoint (possibly unbounded) cyclic operator on a Hilbert space H, and let ϕ be a cyclic vector for A.
The vector ϕ is cyclic for Aα, since it is cyclic for A. Let µα denote the spectral measure of the operator Aα with respect to ϕ.
Open problem: Characterize the singular continuous part of the perturbed operator’s spectral measure in terms of the unperturbed operator.

accepted.question_text: Fix an interval I contained in R and a subset B contained in R. Can one construct a family of measures {mu_beta} such that for every subset J of I the singular part of mu_beta gives J positive mass exactly for parameters beta in B, while the absolutely continuous part of mu_beta gives J positive mass for every beta in R?

evidence.quote: In fact, it possible to produce most any type of singular spectrum in this setting, see [13].

Open question (see [13]): Fix an interval I ⊂ R and subset B ⊂ R. Can one find a family of measures µβ so that (µβ)s(J) > 0 if and only if β ∈ B and (µβ)ac(J) > 0 for all β ∈ R and for every subset J ⊂ I?

accepted.question_text: For the discrete random Schrodinger operator in dimension d >= 2 with independent identically distributed site variables having the uniform law on [-c, c], does the operator have purely singular spectrum for every c > 0? The paper notes that this is already known when c is above a dimension-dependent threshold.
accepted.context_brief: Higher-dimensional Anderson model with uniform disorder

evidence.quote: More concise open problems: If d ≥ 2, does the discrete random Schrödinger operator with P = Π((2c)−1χ[−c,c]dx) have purely singular spectrum for all c > 0.
This statement is known for c above a certain threshold (which is dependent on the dimension).

accepted.question_text: For the discrete random Schrodinger operator in dimension d >= 2 with Bernoulli site variables omega_j taking the values +1 and -1 with equal probability, does the operator have purely singular spectrum?
accepted.context_brief: Higher-dimensional Anderson model with Bernoulli disorder

evidence.quote: The same question is open if ωj = ±1 with equal probability.

accepted.question_text: For finite-rank unitary perturbations of rank n > 1, extend the classical proof of the equivalence between an inner characteristic operator function and purely singular spectrum to the higher-rank setting, and use that extension to derive explicit density formulas for the absolutely continuous part of the spectral measure.

evidence.quote: The equivalency of parts 3) and 4) for general n ∈ N is the main result of [16]. In the case n = 1, the latter equivalence is implied by equation (6.4) below. However, a generalization of the “classical” proof of this equivalence from n = 1 to n > 1 does not seem to be straightforward.

Open problem: It would be useful to extend the original proof to higher rank perturbations, since such an extension could yield the explicit density functions for the absolutely continuous part in the case of finite rank perturbations.

accepted.question_text: For higher-rank unitary perturbations, determine which parts of Aronszajn-Donoghue theory survive after the necessary modifications from the rank-one setting, especially when scalar spectral measures are replaced by vector-valued measures.

evidence.quote: Open direction: In the case of higher rank perturbations, we certainly cannot hope that the singular parts of two perturbed operators’ spectral measures are mutually singular.

However, it would be interesting to see how much of Aronszajn–Donoghue theory can still be true with appropriate changes in the statements. For example, instead of the scalar spectral measure, one would need to consider vector-valued measures.

accepted.question_text: In the Aleksandrov-Clark setting for a non-inner characteristic function theta, interpret formula (6.3) for the adjoint Clark operator and find a useful expression for Phi*_1 f that does not involve the projection P_theta.
accepted.context_brief: Interpretation of the non-inner Clark-operator formula

evidence.quote: Formula (6.3) is not an easy object of study.
One can think of the formula as applying two singular integral operators consecutively.
Open problem: Find an interpretation for this formula. In particular, find a reasonable expression for Φ∗1f that does not involve Pθ.

accepted.question_text: For f in L^2(mu_gamma), can one control the jump across the unit circle of the normalized Cauchy transform of f mu_gamma, and can that control be used to generalize the connection with pseudocontinuation?
accepted.context_brief: Jump behavior of normalized Cauchy transforms

evidence.quote: Open problems: Is it possible to control the jump of the normalized Cauchy transform of fµγ, f ∈ L2(µγ)?
This may be useful for generalizing the connection to pseudocontinuation, see Section 7 below.

accepted.question_text: Determine how the boundary values of the characteristic function theta are related to the spectral behavior of the rank-one unitary perturbations U_gamma.

evidence.quote: Further relationships between the boundary values of θ and the spectral behavior of Uγ would be of interest.

accepted.question_text: Kapustin's restriction is equivalent to the condition integral log |theta| > -infinity. Does that analytic condition admit an interpretation purely in spectral terms?
accepted.context_brief: Spectral meaning of an integrability condition

evidence.quote: Kapustin’s restriction is equivalent to the condition R log |θ| > −∞.
Open question: Does this condition have an interpretation in terms of the spectrum?

needs_review.question_text_raw: Vague open problem: Many conjectures of Anderson localization are unsolved for dimension two and higher.

evidence.quote: Vague open problem: Many conjectures of Anderson localization are unsolved for dimension two and higher.

needs_review.question_text_raw: Vague direction: Results of the above types are related to the Anderson localization conjecture. But even if one does not prove the full conjecture, it would be of interest to find out more about the (almost sure) spectral properties of Anderson-type Hamiltonians via studying rank one and finite rank perturbations; or vice versa.

evidence.quote: Vague direction: Results of the above types are related to the Anderson localization conjecture. But even if one does not prove the full conjecture, it would be of interest to find out more about the (almost sure) spectral properties of Anderson-type Hamiltonians via studying rank one and finite rank perturbations; or vice versa.

needs_review.question_text_raw: Many open questions: What do the different choices of H2 functions have in common? Can we express these conditions in terms of the spectral properties of the class of rank one unitary perturbations of the related non-unitary operator? Is there a canonical way of choosing the H2 functions? What does Kapustin’s result mean in some transcription of the coordinate free model theory? Does Kapustin’s approach extend to rank n unitary perturbations where n > 1? Finally, how are the two meromorphic functions connected to the well-studied objects from Aleksandrov-Clark theory, for example, the normalized Cauchy transform.

evidence.quote: Many open questions: What do the different choices of H2 functions have in common? Can we express these conditions in terms of the spectral properties of the class of rank one unitary perturbations of the related non-unitary operator? Is there a canonical way of choosing the H2 functions? What does Kapustin’s result mean in some transcription of the coordinate free model theory? Does Kapustin’s approach extend to rank n unitary perturbations where n > 1 (certain generalizations of the boundary convergence of pseudocontinuable functions were proved to the vector-valued case, see [29])? Finally, how are the two meromorphic functions connected to the well-studied objects from Aleksandrov–Clark theory, for example, the normalized Cauchy transform.
More precisely, can we relate the pair of functions to the non-tangential jump behavior of the normalized Cauchy transform Cξµ¯ ?

trace.notes: Normalized arXiv abs URL to https://arxiv.org/pdf/1205.4376.pdf and downloaded it to .sisyphus/paper-question-parser/tmp/1205.4376.pdf.

Mapped sections 1-9 from the PDF. Likely open-problem regions were Section 2 (PDF p.3), Section 3 (PDF p.4), Section 4 (PDF pp.5-6), Section 5 (PDF p.8), Section 6 (PDF pp.10-11), Section 7 (PDF p.11), and Section 8 (PDF p.12).
Section 2 yielded one explicit open problem about the singular continuous part of the perturbed spectral measure.
Section 3 yielded one explicit open question about prescribing singular and absolutely continuous parts across a parameter set.
Section 4 yielded two concise explicit Anderson-model problems and two vaguer directions that were not self-contained enough to accept.
Section 5 yielded an explicit higher-rank extension problem and an open direction about extending Aronszajn-Donoghue theory with vector-valued measures.
Section 6 yielded one explicit formula-interpretation problem and two explicit questions about normalized Cauchy transforms and spectral behavior.
Section 7 contained many open questions, but several relied on paper-specific terminology that could not be made self-contained from short local evidence alone.
Section 8 yielded one explicit question about the spectral interpretation of the condition integral log |theta| > -infinity.
Expanded the open problem by inlining the rank-one perturbation setup and the meaning of mu_alpha from nearby evidence.
Rewrote the measure-family question in standalone language while preserving the parameter-set and interval conditions exactly.
Rephrased the open problem in terms of iid uniform site disorder and kept the known-threshold caveat from the source.
Separated the Bernoulli-disorder case from the uniform-disorder case because the source presents it as a distinct open problem.
Made the higher-rank target explicit: extending the classical proof and recovering absolutely continuous density formulas.
Kept the higher-rank Aronszajn-Donoghue question separate because it asks for a broader structural theory rather than a proof extension.
Restated the formula-interpretation problem without source-pointing language and preserved the requirement to eliminate P_theta from the expression.
Rewrote the normalized Cauchy transform question as a standalone problem and preserved its stated link to pseudocontinuation.
Kept the boundary-value question separate from the jump-control question because the source states it as a distinct follow-on problem.
Expanded the condition into a standalone analytic hypothesis before restating the spectral-interpretation question.
